---
title: "3.5 Class Pet and Community Method Challenges"
subtitle: "Part 4: Class Pet Challenge"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Groupwork Coding Challenge {.image-fit}

### Class Pet

![Animal Clinic](../../assets/3-class-creation/3-5-methods-how-to-write-them/animalclinic.png){fig-align="center" width="26%"}

You have been hired to create a software system for the Awesome Animal Clinic.

They would like to keep track of their animal patients.

## Pet Attributes

### Animal clinic records

Here are some attributes of the pets that they would like to track:

::: {.tight-list}
- Name
- Age
- Weight
- Type, such as dog, cat, lizard
- Breed
:::

## Pet Challenge Steps

### Class design requirements

Create a class that keeps track of the attributes for pet records at the animal clinic.

Decide what instance variables are needed and their data types.

Make sure you use `int`, `double`, and `String` data types.

Make the instance variables `private`.

## Pet Methods

### Constructor, getters, setters, and toString

Create:

::: {.tight-list}
- a constructor with many parameters to initialize all the instance variables
- accessor/getter methods for each instance variable
- a `toString` method that returns all the information in a `Pet`
- mutator/setter methods for each instance variable
:::

## Pet Main Method

### Test all methods

In the `main` method, create two `Pet` objects with different values.

Call the constructor, accessor methods, mutator methods, and `toString` methods to test all your code.

## Active Code

### `activecode:: challenge-Pet-Class`

Create a `Pet` class that keeps track of:

::: {.tight-list}
- name
- age
- weight
- type of animal
- breed
:::

Create constructor, getter, setter, and `toString()` methods.

Create two `Pet` objects in the `main` method and test all your methods.

## Pet Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
/**
 * Pet class (complete comments)
 *
 * @author
 * @since
 */
class Pet
{
    // Instance Variables for the name, age, weight, type of animal, and breed of the pet

    // Write a constructor, accessor (get) methods, mutator (set) methods)
    // and a toString method. Use good commenting.

    // Don't forget to complete the main method in the TesterClass below!
}

public class TesterClass
{
    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // Create 2 Pet objects and test all your methods

    }
}
```
:::

## Pet Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The Runestone tests check:

::: {.tight-list}
- at least one constructor for `Pet`
- five private instance variables
- at least five accessor/getter methods
- `public String toString()`
- at least five mutator/setter methods
- at least two `Pet` objects created in `main`
- `main` prints information for two `Pet` objects
:::

## Community Class Extension

### Continue your own class

In the last lessons, you came up with a class of your own choice relevant to your community.

Copy your class with its three instance variables, constructor, and its `print()` and `main` methods from lesson 3.4 into the active code exercise.

## Community Methods

### Add methods to your class

Create:

::: {.tight-list}
- accessor/get methods for each instance variable
- mutator/set methods for each instance variable
- a `toString` method that returns all information in the instance variables
- an additional method for your class that takes a parameter
:::

## Method With a Parameter

### Example idea

For example, there could be a `print` method with arguments that indicate how you want to print out the information.

`print(format)` could print the data according to an argument that is `"plain"` or `"table"`.

You may also come up with another creative method for your class.

Use these methods in the `main` method to test them.

Make sure you use good commenting.

## Community Active Code

### `activecode:: community-challenge3`

Copy your class from lesson 3.4.

Add get, set, `toString`, and a method that takes a parameter.

For example, there could be a `print` method with arguments that indicate how you want to print out the information, `print(format)` where format is `"plain"` or `"table"`.

## Community Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class          // Add your class name here!
{
    // 1. Copy your class instance variables, constructor, and print()

    // 2. Create accessor (get) and mutator (set) methods for each of the instance variables.

    // 3. Create a toString() method that returns all the information in the instance variables.
    // 4.  Add a method for your class that takes a parameter.
    // For example, there could be a print method with arguments that indicate
    // how you want to print out the information, print(format) where format is "plain" or "table".

    // 5. Test all the methods in the main method.
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
       // Construct an object of your class


       // call the object's methods

    }
}
```
:::

## Community Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The Runestone tests check:

::: {.tight-list}
- at least three private instance variables
- a constructor with three parameters
- `print` output longer than 15 characters
- `main` output with at least three lines of text
- at least three accessor/get methods
- at least three mutator/set methods
- `public String toString()`
- a `public void print(String` method
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- design `Pet` instance variables using `int`, `double`, and `String`
- keep all `Pet` instance variables private
- write constructor, getter, setter, and `toString` methods for the clinic record
- create and test two `Pet` objects in `main`
- carry forward the community class from lesson 3.4
- add get/set methods, `toString`, and a parameterized method to the community class
:::

## End

### 3.5 complete

Next topic: 3.6 Methods: Passing and Returning References of an Object.
